does: This paper proposes Visual-Noise Guided In-Context Distillation (VGID), a method for removing sensitive or restricted knowledge from multimodal large language models without full retraining. The approach combines in-context unlearning with visual noise injection and knowledge distillation to simultaneously reduce model memorization at the parameter level and mitigate visual conditioning effects. VGID operates by adding carefully designed noise to visual inputs during inference while using distillation to guide the model away from generating undesirable outputs. The method aims to achieve a balance between effective knowledge removal and preservation of general model capabilities on unrelated tasks.
connects: This work builds on the growing field of machine unlearning, which emerged as a response to privacy concerns and the need to remove specific knowledge from trained models. The paper addresses limitations of prior approaches: training-based unlearning methods that sacrifice model utility through parameter updates, and training-free in-context unlearning that fails to modify parameters and remains vulnerable to attacks. It extends in-context learning techniques to the multimodal setting, recognizing that visual inputs present unique challenges in MLLMs compared to text-only models. The work also draws on knowledge distillation approaches to guide model behavior and incorporates adversarial noise injection techniques adapted for the unlearning objective.
enables: This work opens pathways for developing more robust privacy-preserving mechanisms in multimodal AI systems. It enables safer deployment of MLLMs in sensitive domains where certain knowledge must be restricted while maintaining utility for general tasks. The approach could extend to other multimodal architectures and larger model scales, potentially supporting regulatory compliance for models handling proprietary or restricted information. Future work might combine VGID with certified unlearning guarantees, explore interactions between text and visual noise injection, develop stronger defenses against reverse-engineering attacks on unlearned knowledge, and establish formal benchmarks for evaluating unlearning effectiveness in multimodal settings while measuring preservation of related but acceptable knowledge.
